description: The abstract mathematical theory of objects and morphisms between them. Provides a unifying language for all of mathematics, capturing universal constructions, functors, and natural transformations.

concepts.name: Category
concepts.description: A category C consists of: a class of objects Ob(C), for each pair (A,B) a set Hom(A,B) of morphisms, composition ∘ (associative), and identity morphisms 1_A. Examples: Set (sets + functions), Grp (groups + homomorphisms), Top (spaces + continuous maps), Vect_k (vector spaces + linear maps).
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.1; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.1

concepts.name: Types of Morphisms
concepts.description: Monomorphism (mono): gf=hf ⟹ g=h (left cancellable; generalizes injective). Epimorphism (epi): fg=fh ⟹ g=h (right cancellable; generalizes surjective). Isomorphism: morphism with a two-sided inverse. Endomorphism: f:A→A. Automorphism: isomorphism A→A.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §I.5

concepts.name: Functor
concepts.description: A structure-preserving map between categories: F sends objects to objects and morphisms to morphisms, preserving composition and identities. Contravariant functor: reverses arrows. Examples: forgetful functors, free functors, homology, π₁.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §I.6; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, §1.3

concepts.name: Natural Transformation
concepts.description: A natural transformation η:F⟹G between functors F,G:C→D assigns to each object X a morphism η_X:F(X)→G(X) such that for all f:X→Y, η_Y∘F(f)=G(f)∘η_X (naturality). The 'right notion of morphism' between functors.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §I.4; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, §1.4

concepts.name: Functor Category
concepts.description: The functor category [C,D] (also written D^C) has functors F:C→D as objects and natural transformations as morphisms. Composition is pointwise. Allows treating functors as objects in a category — foundation of higher category theory.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §II.6

concepts.name: Yoneda Lemma
concepts.description: Nat(Hom(A,−), F) ≅ F(A) naturally in A and F. An object A is completely determined by the functor Hom(A,−) it represents. The Yoneda embedding C→[Cᵒᵖ,Set] is fully faithful — the deepest elementary result of category theory.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §III.2; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.2

concepts.name: Representable Functor
concepts.description: F:C→Set is representable iff F≅Hom(A,−) for some object A (the representing object). The universal element is the element u∈F(A) corresponding to id_A. Representability captures universal properties.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §III.2

concepts.name: Limits and Colimits
concepts.description: A limit of a diagram D:J→C is a universal cone over D. Colimit: universal cocone. Products and coproducts are limits/colimits of discrete diagrams. Equalizers, pullbacks, pushouts are special limits. Functors that preserve limits are continuous.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.V; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.3

concepts.name: Equalizer and Coequalizer
concepts.description: Equalizer of f,g:A→B: an object E with morphism e:E→A such that f∘e=g∘e, universal with this property. In Set: E = {a∈A : f(a)=g(a)}. Coequalizer: colimit; in Set: quotient by the equivalence relation generated by {(f(a),g(a))}.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §V.2

concepts.name: Pullback and Pushout
concepts.description: Pullback of f:A→C, g:B→C: the limit of the diagram A→C←B. In Set: {(a,b)∈A×B : f(a)=g(b)}. Pushout: colimit of A←C→B. Pullbacks compute fiber products; pushouts glue spaces. Essential in topology, algebraic geometry, type theory.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §II.3

concepts.name: Adjunction
concepts.description: F⊣G (F left adjoint to G) iff Hom_D(FA,B)≅Hom_C(A,GB) naturally. Unit η:1_C⟹GF, counit ε:FG⟹1_D. Adjoints are ubiquitous: free/forgetful, product/exponential, direct/inverse image, colimit/constant. 'Adjoint functors arise everywhere' (Mac Lane).
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.IV; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.4

concepts.name: Monad
concepts.description: A monad on C: a functor T:C→C with unit η:1⟹T and multiplication μ:T²⟹T satisfying associativity and unit laws. Every adjunction F⊣G determines a monad GF. Algebras over a monad generalize many algebraic structures. Monads appear in functional programming (Haskell Maybe, IO, List).
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.VI; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.5

concepts.name: Tensor Product (Categorical)
concepts.description: A monoidal category (C,⊗,I) has a tensor product ⊗:C×C→C and unit I satisfying associativity and unit laws up to natural isomorphism. Examples: (Set,×,{*}), (Ab,⊗_ℤ,ℤ), (Vect,⊗,k). Symmetric monoidal: A⊗B≅B⊗A.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.VII

concepts.name: Topos
concepts.description: An elementary topos is a category with finite limits, exponentials (function objects), and a subobject classifier Ω. Generalizes set-like mathematics; internal logic is intuitionistic. Grothendieck toposes: sheaves on a site. Bridges logic, geometry, and algebra.
references: Mac Lane & Moerdijk, Sheaves in Geometry and Logic; Goldblatt, Topoi: the Categorial Analysis of Logic

concepts.name: Higher Category Theory
concepts.description: 2-category: categories as objects, functors as morphisms, natural transformations as 2-morphisms. ∞-categories (quasicategories, Segal spaces): homotopy-coherent structures. Homotopy Type Theory (HoTT): types are spaces, proofs are paths. Active research frontier.
references: Lurie, Higher Topos Theory; Riehl, Verity, Elements of ∞-Category Theory; Univalent Foundations, HoTT Book
